utterances: Well, you know, there's just a lot of concern, Audie, about this surge in fighting in the east of Ukraine.
The U.S.
and Europe say Russia has been feeling this conflict, shipping heavy weapons.
And the Western policy of sanctions just hasn't changed Putin's calculations here.
So everyone's trying to figure out what his end game is, how to get the Russians and the separatists to go back to the cease-fire lines that they agreed to last year.
And no one really wants to relitigate that peace deal.
And everyone seems to be sort of testing each other at the moment.
Why are the leaders of the two, I mean, leading countries in western Europe flying to Moscow to meet Putin?
Well, we're told that Putin has come up with some new peace proposals, which one Western diplomat described as a cynical attempt to get out from those commitments that the Russians made last year.
France and Germany are offering counterproposals.
And remember, this all comes against this backdrop of a separatist offensive - the separatists and their Russian backers seem to think that the Europeans just want any diplomatic solution.
Kerry says Ukraine has already offered what the Russians say they want, which is more autonomy for these predominantly ethnic Russian areas of eastern Ukraine.
And Kerry says the Russians have to do their part and pull out troops and tanks and seal off that border.
If this peace plan doesn't work, what can Secretary Kerry and U.S.
allies do to help Ukraine stop the Russian-backed separatists?
Well, we know what they're not planning to do - at least not yet - and that is ship weapons to Kiev - the weapons that Kiev has been seeking.

Kerry came with $16 million in fresh humanitarian aid for the East, but his aides have made clear that the Obama administration is still weighing this idea and has not yet decided to ship things like anti-tank missiles, something experts in Washington have been talking about all week.
The other thing they're talking about is stepping up sanctions, and that's something we'll likely hear in the weeks ahead.
And this is all happening on the eve of the annual security conference in Munich where Vice President Biden and John Kerry are expected to lay out U.S.
security concerns for the coming year, correct?
That's right.
And the two of them are also having a meeting with Ukraine's president who will be there along with Chancellor Merkel who will be back from Russia.
Kerry is also going to be meeting with his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov.
So this is what one State Department official likes to say, live diplomacy.

Another big concern that we're likely to hear about at this Munich conference is Russia's aggressive actions in the skies over Europe - planes flying with their transponders off and other tests that NATO countries see as provocative.
Just today NATO said it plans a huge increase in its rapid reaction force.
It's supposed to deter Russian aggression, and we're going to hear from NATO's secretary general in Munich as well.
That's NPR's Michele Kelemen, traveling with Secretary John Kerry.
Michele, thanks so much.
Thank you, Audie.
For more on the politics within the Palestinian territories, we turn now to NPR's Anthony Kuhn in Gaza.
He reports that the current conflict has helped unite Palestine's various factions and strengthened Hamas' domestic political position.
A video on the Internet shows a concealed metal trapdoor in the ground opening automatically.
On the underside of the door are missile tubes and the flag of the Islamic Jihad movement.
(SOUNDBITE OF ROCKETS LAUNCHING)
The point of the video is to show that Islamic Jihad is contributing to the resistance against Israeli occupation of Palestinian lands.
Islamic Jihad shares roots with Hamas.
But Omar Shaban, director of the Gaza-based think-tank Pal-Think, says the two groups are involved in a sort of subtle political competition for leadership of the Islamist camp.
Hamas needs to be in a delicate process with these parties.
Hamas cannot crack down against them, and need to please them from time to time.
But at the same time, Hamas wants from others, like Islamic Jihad, to respect its power.
In fact, many of the rockets fired at Israel in recent years were launched by Islamic Jihad, not Hamas.
Israel has held Hamas responsible for keeping other factions from launching missile attacks, which Shaban argues Hamas has tried to do.
Hamas tried very hard to keep the truce and to work with other faction - sometimes by force, sometimes by negotiation - in order to bring them into the truce, as well.
Sometimes Hamas even arrested some of the leaders of other groups who were trying to violate the truce.
Analysts say Hamas has two mutually contradictory roles.
One is to keep the peace as Gaza's ruling authority.
The other is to fight and lead the resistance against Israel.
Thou el-Foqaar Sweerjo is a central committee member of the leftist Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.
He says now that Hamas is doing more fighting, it's restored Hamas' credibility in his party's eyes.
(Through translator) We always thought before this escalation that Hamas believed in the theory of resistance, but that its role as a governing institution prevented it from practicing it.
But this escalation has forced Hamas to practice resistance, and now we stand with them.
Well, I think we have to be intelligent, as difficult as that is.
Nuclear proliferation is inevitable, because you can not ban knowledge.
You can only hope to discourage, to monitor--moral persuasion through the use of monitoring--and the use of the development of nuclear power and hope that people will never use it.
You have to make those people, who dare to use nuclear bombs and things like that, international pariahs.
But we have the specter that there's a possibility that the United States--in answer to the question, what do about Iran's nuclear development--what are you going to do, nuke them?
No. Meanwhile…
Well, that's actually a proposal that's on the table, to nuke nuclear bunkers.
It's a proposal on the table, but it is immoral.
And in the meanwhile, the question for Americans is how do you spell carpool?
You're going to have a huge increase in price of oil.
The only country now saving us is Saudi Arabia.
Our investment in Iraq is not paying off.
The oil companies—Exxon is quoting George M.
Cohen.
My mother thanks you.
My father thanks you.
My sister thanks you, and surely I thank you.
Oil prices are out of control.
Ladies and gentlemen, as they say at the movies, please turn off your cell phones for the duration of the show.
But Michael, you bring up a great point, which is that Iran is one of the top oil producing countries in the world--and right now, we're close to hitting $3.00 for a gallon of gas.
Frankly, here in California, we are hitting $3.00 for a gallon of gas.
And I just filled up my car and I was like, hey, hey, what happened?
My tank's not full for the same amount of money I usually pay.
So you say carpool.
Are Americans finally ready to scale down on the SUVs or sign up for the, you know, hybrid cars?
Or are we just going to continue driving our Hummers and paying five or six dollars a gallon, at some point?
Well, ready or not, the future isn't what it used to be.
And people will find it very costly to get to work, particularly if they are living in suburbia or exurbia, as a lot of people are--unless the jobs move out to exurbia.
So you're going to have to carpool.
You're going to have to pay more in terms of the price of fuel for your homes.
People who live in co-ops and things like that will pay more through surcharges.
We'll pay more in airplane tickets--they will skyrocket.
It's going to be very costly, this cost, all because we haven't developed alternatives to oil, yet.
Glenn…
Well, this may…
Oh, go ahead, E.R.

I was going to say, this may be the incentive that we need, once the magical three dollars creeps up to maybe even a magical four dollar mark, maybe that's the incentive we need to develop alternatives to fuel, the gasoline.
But I'm going to say this, from the environmentalists' perspective, if fewer people are driving, that's not a bad thing.
Glenn, I wanted you to chime in on an economic tip.
Americans have not seemed to be highly motivated so far by gas prices, in terms of changing our patterns of driving or changing the kind of vehicles we drive.
Now, just want to mention that Exxon Mobil has topped the Fortune 500 ranking of the biggest U.S.
companies, for the first time in a few years, thanks to the soaring oil prices during 2005.
They brought in $340 billion in earnings, $36 billion in profits.
Some people have argued that, during a time like this, during this past winter--they should have kicked back some money to help people who were impoverished and who were on hard times with fuel oil.
But basically, how do Americans respond to something like gas prices?
Is there a correlation between gas prices and behavior, especially around automobiles?
Yes.
Prices go up.
People drive less, and they find ways of economizing.
Actually, in my opinion, the price of fossil fuels in the United States is too low.
It's way too low.
We need to have a very substantial additional federal excise tax on gasoline.
We could use some of those monies to allay our budget deficits and so forth, but the main point would be, people are not going to conserve unless the market forces them, through higher prices.
We've seen our automobiles get bigger and less fuel efficient in recent years, because of the rise of the SUV.
The price of gasoline in Europe is twice what it is here in the United States, today.
The gasoline prices need to be higher.
I understand that that will impose hardship on people.
We should be setting up programs to try to protect those who are needy against the increase, but the only way people are going to conserve is if the market sends them the signal.

And if we want to protect our environment, and if we want to not transfer hundreds of billions of dollars to our political enemies around the world, we need to raise gasoline prices and other fossil fuel prices in the United States.
Ouch!
Ouch!
But Glenn, if you were a senator, you would be voted out in a hot, buttered second.
(Laughing) Yes, I understand it…
Where's the political will for that?
But he would go down in history as someone who had vision.
If he weren't tenured, he might be kicked out of Brown, too.
(Laughing)
Yes…
Sorry, but I'm just telling you the facts.
Yes, we'll see if that happens.
Well, let's move on to another political issue.
The Providence city council voted on Thursday, to pull its money out of companies that do business in the Sudan--becoming the first American city to divest from the African nation.
States including Maine, Illinois, and New Jersey have pulled their investments from Sudan, and several universities have divested, including Brown, in Providence.
Now, is this a parallel to South Africa where there was a large divestment movement that helped end apartheid.
Do you think that we're seeing the beginning of a ground swell, E.R?
What?
Where were the standards of conduct?
This was void for vagueness.
My response to anybody who wants to go to this kind of a college is, Oh Lordy.
But look, this is a Baptist--I mean E.R.
Shipp is right--this is a Baptist college.
It's a private college.
It's not a public college.
I think you are.
It's interesting that this movement, as with the South African movement, started relatively slowly with student activism, and that's what's really pushing this particular movement right now.
Students across the country are persuading their universities and various components of university communities, to divest.
In some cases, it seems like a very small amount of money, given the money that these corporations have.
For example, now the money that Brown would be—or that Providence, I'm sorry--would be pulling out of this company Alcatel is about $800,000.
Well, it's not a lot of money, but it makes a dramatic point, and it's a point that gets international attention, which then continues the movement for divesting and for putting pressure on Sudan.
There is, of course, the ongoing genocide there.
Two million people displaced.
One hundred eighty thousand or more killed.
Michael, what other strategies could people look at in a situation like this?
You have to look, again, at the United Nations and the Security Council.
I don't what these guys are doing over there, other than talking.
And where is the leadership from the United States about the genocide that's going on there.
I'm glad to see this, sort of, on-the-ground, grass roots movement happening, because people have to start paying attention.
If it's going to take economic investment to do that, good.

I do see parallels with the divestment campaign against South Africa--a campaign that was also led by religious and civil rights community--resisted by major big corporations and by big foundations like Rockefeller and Ford.
And, because you remember, in those days, you have the sovereign principles and what I call gradualism.
But gradualism was eventually rejected because there was a moral sway that came to the nation because of activism.
And people want to make a statement about apartheid in South Africa.
